Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every day, millions of people ask ChatGPT millions of questions. Whether it’s writing code, debugging applications, learning a new concept, or simply answering everyday questions, ChatGPT responds within seconds.
But have you ever wondered what actually happens after you press Enter?
How does ChatGPT understand your question?
How does it generate a completely new response instead of copying one from the internet?
In this article, we’ll take a journey inside ChatGPT and explore every important step — from the moment you type a prompt until the final response appears on your screen.
Let’s get started.

What Is an LLM?
LLM stands for Large Language Model.
In simple terms, an LLM is an AI model trained on massive amounts of text data. It learns patterns, grammar, facts, and relationships between words, allowing it to generate the next token (word or part of a word) based on the input it receives
What problems do LLMs solve?
Large Language Models bridge the gap between human language and computer processing. Instead of requiring people to learn programming languages or use complex search queries, LLMs understand natural language and generate human-like responses.
Popular examples of LLMs?
There are many Large Language Models available today. Some of the most popular ones include:

• ChatGPT (OpenAI)

• Gemini (Google)

• Claude (Anthropic)

• Blackbox AI

• GitHub Copilot (Microsoft)
Each model has its own strengths, but they all use the same core idea of predicting the next token based on the given input.
Common applications in daily life?
I use LLM-powered applications almost every day. My favorites are ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini. They help me with:

• Learning new concepts

• Writing and improving content

• Coding and debugging

• Research

• Brainstorming ideas

• Increasing productivity in my daily work

What Happens When You Send a Message to ChatGPT?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 1 — Prompt
Everything begins with your prompt.
A prompt is simply the text you type into ChatGPT.
Example:
Helloor
Explain JavaScript Closures.At this stage, the prompt is still plain human language.
Step 2 — Tokenization
Computers don’t process entire sentences directly.
The first step is Tokenization.
A tokenizer breaks your prompt into smaller pieces called tokens.
For example,
Hello, how are you?might become
["Hello", ",", " how", " are", " you", "?"]Notice that a token isn’t always a complete word. It can be:

• A word

• Part of a word

• Punctuation

• Numbers

• Symbols
This allows the model to efficiently process text.
Step 3 — Token IDs
Tokens are still text.
The tokenizer converts every token into a unique numerical ID.
For example,
Hellomight become
15496These numbers are what the neural network actually processes.
Step 4 — Embeddings
Numbers alone don’t carry meaning.
Each token ID is transformed into a high-dimensional vector called an embedding.
An embedding represents the meaning of a token mathematically.
Words with similar meanings tend to have similar embeddings.
For example,
King
Queen
Prince
Princesswill have embeddings that are closer together than unrelated words like:
King
Pizza
Laptop
BananaEmbeddings allow the model to understand semantic relationships.
Step 5 — Positional Embeddings
Now imagine these two sentences:
Dog bites man.Man bites dog.Both sentences contain exactly the same words.
Yet their meanings are completely different.
Why?
Because word order matters.
Embeddings alone don’t tell the model where each token appears.
To solve this, ChatGPT adds positional embeddings, which encode the position of every token in the sentence. This helps the model understand sequence and context.
Step 6 — The Transformer
Now the embeddings enter the Transformer.
The Transformer is the neural network architecture that powers GPT models.
Instead of reading words one by one like older neural networks, the Transformer can look at all tokens simultaneously and understand how they relate to each other.
This is what makes GPT extremely powerful.
Step 7 — Self-Attention
One of the most important innovations inside the Transformer is Self-Attention.
Self-attention allows every token to determine which other tokens are important.
Consider the sentence:
The animal didn’t cross the street because it was tired.
What does it refer to?
Self-attention helps the model understand that it refers to the animal, not the street.
This enables ChatGPT to understand context instead of looking at words independently.
Step 8 — Feed Forward Network
After self-attention identifies the important relationships between tokens, each token passes through a Feed Forward Network (FFN).
The FFN performs additional mathematical transformations that help the model refine its understanding and extract more complex patterns.
This attention → feed forward process is repeated many times across multiple Transformer layers.
Step 9 — Predicting the Next Token
After processing the context, the model predicts the probability of every possible next token.
For example,
The capital of France isThe model may assign probabilities like:
Paris 96%
London 2%
Berlin 1%
Tokyo 1%The token with the highest (or an appropriately sampled) probability is selected.
Step 10 — Sampling
The highest-probability token isn’t always chosen.
Depending on settings like temperature, Top-k, and Top-p sampling, the model may choose different tokens to make responses more creative or more deterministic.
This is why the same prompt can sometimes produce different responses.
Step 11 — Repeat the Process
Once one token is generated, it becomes part of the context.
The model repeats the entire process again.
Predict one token
↓Add it to the sentence
↓Predict the next token
↓Repeat...This happens extremely quickly, allowing ChatGPT to stream responses one token at a time.
It doesn’t generate the entire paragraph first and then display it. Instead, it predicts and streams tokens continuously until the response is complete.
Step 12 — End of Sequence (EOS)
Eventually, the model predicts a special token called EOS (End of Sequence).
EOS tells the model that the response is complete and generation should stop.
Final Output
The generated tokens are converted back into readable text and streamed to your screen.
What looks like an instant conversation is actually the result of billions of mathematical operations happening in just a fraction of a second.
Does ChatGPT Search the Internet for Every Answer?
A common misconception is that ChatGPT searches the internet every time you ask a question.
That’s not how the base model works.
Instead, it generates responses by predicting the next token based on patterns learned during training. However, some versions of ChatGPT can also use web browsing tools when needed to access current or real-time information.
Conclusion
The next time you ask ChatGPT a question, remember that an incredible journey is happening behind the scenes.
Your prompt is tokenized, converted into numerical IDs, transformed into embeddings, enriched with positional information, processed through multiple Transformer layers using self-attention and feed-forward networks, and finally decoded into a response — one token at a time.
Although it feels like ChatGPT understands language the way humans do, it’s actually performing sophisticated mathematical computations to predict what comes next.
And that’s what makes Large Language Models one of the most fascinating breakthroughs in modern AI.
